reviewerResults.persona: Small startup CTO with low to medium AWS experience. Cares most about time-to-production and shipping quickly without getting trapped in AWS complexity.
strengths: Strong 'When to use' / 'When not to use' section with a concrete comparison table — helps a startup CTO make a fast decision without reading the whole page.
CodeBlock usage is correct throughout — all Stacktape config examples use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]}/>, application code uses fenced blocks, no forbidden patterns.
Spot instances section is excellent: clear guidance on when to use vs. avoid, paired with the retry tip. This is exactly the practical tradeoff framing the audience needs.
GPU section is concise and grounded — confirmed by source code that Stacktape uses p4d and g5 instance types. Custom Dockerfile packaging recommendation is appropriate.
Triggers table is comprehensive and scannable — covers all 10 supported event types with one-line use case descriptions, matching the source types exactly.

STP_TRIGGER_EVENT_DATA, STP_CURRENT_ATTEMPT, and STP_MAXIMUM_ATTEMPTS environment variables are all verified in source code (utils.ts:324-337) — good grounded detail that helps the reader write checkpoint/resume logic.
problems: Retry wait time example is factually incorrect. The page claims waits of '10s, 20s, 30s, 40s' for retryIntervalSeconds: 10 and retryIntervalMultiplier: 2. The actual source code formula (utils.ts:363) is `retryIntervalSeconds * attemptIndex * retryIntervalMultiplier`, producing waits of 20s, 40s, 60s, 80s. The stated formula in the prose is correct but the example numbers contradict it.

Pricing section claims 'You pay only for EC2 compute time while the job runs' without concrete pricing data. The provided pricing source file groups batch-job under Fargate pricing (which is incorrect per source code), so there is no grounded EC2 pricing to cite. The page should either omit specific pricing claims or note that costs depend on the instance type AWS provisions.

Missing coverage of container.environment — a core configuration property. The page shows connectTo for injected variables but never shows how to set custom environment variables (e.g., API keys, feature flags) via the container.environment property. This is a common first task for the audience.

Missing mention of dev mode support. The types (DevModeCapableResourceType) include batch-job, meaning batch jobs work with `stacktape dev`. For a startup CTO iterating quickly, this is a high-value feature omission.

Missing mention of log forwarding. BatchJobLogging extends LogForwardingBase, meaning logs can be forwarded to external destinations (Datadog, etc.). The Logging section only mentions CloudWatch and retentionDays.
mandatoryFixes: Fix the retry wait time example: with retryIntervalSeconds: 10 and retryIntervalMultiplier: 2, the correct waits (per utils.ts:363) are 20s, 40s, 60s, 80s — not 10s, 20s, 30s, 40s. Either fix the numbers or change the example config values to produce the stated sequence (e.g., retryIntervalSeconds: 10, retryIntervalMultiplier: 1 → 10s, 20s, 30s, 40s).

Rework the pricing context line. The current claim ('You pay only for EC2 compute time') is directionally correct but has no grounded pricing data from the source files. Either remove specific cost claims (Spot 'up to 90%' is fine — it's from the JSDoc) or add a note that costs depend on instance type and duration. Do not cite Fargate pricing for batch jobs — the source code confirms EC2 compute environments.

Add a brief example or mention of container.environment for setting custom environment variables. This is a first-class property on BatchJobContainer and a common configuration need the audience will hit immediately.
optionalImprovements: Add a one-line mention and link for dev mode support — batch jobs are listed as DevModeCapableResourceType, and rapid local iteration matters to the target audience.
Mention log forwarding briefly in the Logging section (BatchJobLogging extends LogForwardingBase) with a link to the log forwarding docs page, for teams using Datadog/Splunk/etc.

Consider adding a short note on the MinvCpus: 0 / MaxvCpus: 1000 behavior from the source — it's useful context that AWS Batch scales the compute environment to zero when no jobs are running, which reinforces the 'pay only when running' message.

The basic example handler uses top-level await (`const data = await fetchData(event)`) without mentioning that the script needs ESM or a wrapper. Consider wrapping in an async IIFE or adding a note about module format.
The 'Retries and timeout' section has an H3 for 'Timeout' that is only one sentence. Consider folding it into the parent section intro or the retries subsection to avoid a near-empty heading.

reviewerResults.persona: SMB engineering manager with medium to high AWS knowledge. Cares about long-term maintainability, team clarity, operational fit, technically correct Stacktape class-based config examples, and valid MDX component usage.
strengths: Strong page shape: the reader gets use cases, alternatives, a basic example, resources, retries, triggers, connections, logging, and API reference in a logical order.
Stacktape configuration examples use class-based resources inside `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]}/>` and avoid forbidden YAML/plain-object config patterns.
The Spot guidance is useful and mostly grounded in the source: restartable workloads, non-repeatable side effects, retries, SIGTERM, and 120-second shutdown window.
The resource sizing section includes a practical memory-sizing tip that is directly supported by the source comments.
problems: Several technical statements are too broad or unsupported by the provided sources, especially around AWS Batch internals, EC2-only compute, GPU instance families, and Lambda limits.

The pricing context conflicts with the provided pricing source: the source says the distilled pricing is for Fargate used by `batch-job`, while the draft says EC2 compute time and names GPU instance families without sourced prices.

The “up to 10 GB” Lambda comparison is wrong or at least unsupported in this source set; it reads like memory, while Lambda’s commonly known 10 GB figure is ephemeral storage, not memory. The provided sources do not support this comparison.

The retry wait example is inconsistent with the source description. The source says `retryIntervalSeconds: 5` and multiplier `2` gives waits of `5s, 10s, 20s`; the draft says `10s, 20s, 30s, 40s` for interval `10` and multiplier `2`, which is not exponential backoff.

The basic handler assumes `STP_TRIGGER_EVENT_DATA` is always present and parseable. The source supports that event payloads are available when triggered, but the example may fail for invocation paths without event data unless guarded or scoped more narrowly.
The trigger table lists all supported event types, which is useful, but it is thin on decision guidance for an engineering manager deciding between schedule, queue, HTTP, and event bus triggers.
The first paragraph is 60+ words and slightly overloaded; it should stay within the 40-60 word SEO requirement and avoid ungrounded implementation claims.
mandatoryFixes: Revise pricing to match the provided pricing source. Do not state EC2-only pricing or GPU rates unless the source supports it; use the available Fargate ballpark context and Spot interruption/savings wording from the type source.
Remove or narrow unsupported AWS Batch/EC2/GPU-instance claims. For example, avoid naming `p4d`/`g5` unless a source file explicitly supports those instance families.
Fix the retry backoff explanation so it matches the source: multiplier-based waits should be exponential, e.g. `5s, 10s, 20s` for `retryIntervalSeconds: 5` and `retryIntervalMultiplier: 2`.
Remove or reword the unsupported `up to 10 GB` Lambda comparison.
Shorten the opening paragraph to 40-60 words and keep it self-contained without unsourced implementation detail.
optionalImprovements: Use `label: 'TypeScript'` for CodeBlock tabs if the current docs pipeline expects that exact visible tab label; the example page uses `stacktape.ts`, so verify the local convention before changing.

Add one compact paragraph under “Triggers” that recommends common trigger choices: schedule for periodic work, SQS for buffered async work, S3 for object processing, HTTP API for explicit on-demand starts, and event bus for routed domain events.
Mention that `container.environment` can use `$ResourceParam()` or `$Secret()` for runtime configuration, since that is directly supported by the source and useful for real jobs.
Clarify that `timeout` is in seconds and that timed-out jobs can be retried when `retryConfig` is set, which is source-backed and operationally important.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-structured and covers the batch-job resource comprehensively. Most factual claims are grounded in source. Two high-severity issues found: (1) the retry backoff example numbers (10s, 20s, 30s, 40s) do not match the stated formula with retryIntervalSeconds: 10 and retryIntervalMultiplier: 2 — the correct values would be 20s, 40s, 60s, 80s; (2) there is an inconsistency between the pricing source file (which categorizes batch-job under Fargate) and the actual implementation (which uses EC2 compute environments) — the opening paragraph correctly says EC2 but the pricing file contradicts this, creating confusion. The GPU instance types (p4d, g5) and all three injected environment variables (STP_TRIGGER_EVENT_DATA, STP_CURRENT_ATTEMPT, STP_MAXIMUM_ATTEMPTS) were confirmed in the codebase. Trigger types, defaults, and logging configuration all match the source accurately.

The draft is generally aligned with the BatchJob type surface, but it contains several factual problems around compute underpinnings/pricing, retry timing, supported packaging class names, trigger behavior, Lambda comparisons, and unsupported runtime environment variables. The highest-risk issues are claims that batch jobs use EC2/AWS Batch instance provisioning while the provided pricing source says batch-job uses Fargate, and a retry-backoff formula that contradicts the type comments.

suggestions: **Expand the Timeout section.** It's currently a single sentence with no defaults, no max value, and no code example. Add the default timeout (if any), the maximum allowed value, and a short code snippet showing `timeout: 3600`. A stub section hurts AEO because an AI engine may extract it as the 'answer' for 'batch job timeout' queries and deliver almost nothing useful.

**Add an FAQ section** with 3-5 questions the page already answers but that would benefit from explicit Q&A markup: 'How do Spot instances work with batch jobs?', 'What triggers can start a batch job?', 'How do I connect a batch job to a database?', 'How do I retry a failed batch job?', 'What GPU instances does AWS Batch support?'. These map directly to long-tail queries and are extractable by AI answer engines.

**Shorten the heading 'Connecting to other resources'** to 'Resource connections' or 'Connect to databases and storage'. At 32 characters it's the longest H2 and reads more like a sentence than a label. Shorter headings improve scan-ability and match search query patterns better.

**Verify the retry math example.** The text says waits are '10s, 20s, 30s, 40s' using the formula `retryIntervalSeconds × attempt index × retryIntervalMultiplier`, but plugging in the values (10 × 1 × 2 = 20, 10 × 2 × 2 = 40, 10 × 3 × 2 = 60, 10 × 4 × 2 = 80) produces different numbers. Incorrect concrete specifics are worse than vague wording for AEO — AI engines will confidently repeat wrong numbers.

**Add a one-sentence architecture note to the opening section** explaining that Stacktape uses AWS Batch for compute provisioning and Step Functions for retry orchestration. This gives AI engines a concise 'how it works' extract without requiring readers to piece it together from the Retries section later in the page.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is factually solid. All key technical claims — trigger types, retry defaults, environment variable injection, Spot behavior, logging configuration, and dev mode support — are verified against source code. The retry backoff formula and example values correctly match the runtime implementation (linear: base × attempt × multiplier), even though the JSDoc misleadingly describes it as 'exponential backoff'. The five issues found are: (1) an FAQ section that is explicitly forbidden by the style guide, (2) CodeBlock tab labels using 'stacktape.ts' instead of the required 'TypeScript', (3) a JSDoc/code discrepancy around the retry backoff description that could confuse readers, (4) missing concrete Fargate pricing figures that are available in the source, and (5) an ambiguous 'Resources' section heading that collides with the Stacktape config `resources` concept. No high-severity factual errors were found.

The draft is broadly aligned with the BatchJob type surface, but it contains several unsupported or incorrect operational claims, one invalid API/reference component concern, and multiple docs-style violations that would fail the stated rules. The largest factual risks are around pricing, retry backoff math, logging placement, dev mode behavior, and HTTP-trigger wording.

suggestions: Answer block: Strong. The opening paragraph (45 words) defines what a batch job is, lists concrete use cases, and states the pay-per-run pricing model. It works well as a Featured Snippet extraction. Consider adding 'on AWS' to the first sentence for geographic/platform specificity: 'A batch job runs a container on AWS to completion and exits…'

Heading 'Resources' is too generic — rename to 'CPU, memory, and GPU' so the heading itself carries search signal for queries like 'batch job GPU configuration' or 'batch job memory settings'. The current H3 'GPU' would then become redundant as a standalone heading; fold it into the parent section or rename it to 'GPU configuration' for clarity.

The retry math explanation ('Each wait = retryIntervalSeconds × attempt number × retryIntervalMultiplier. With the config above, waits between retries are 20s, 40s, 60s, 80s.') is good but would be stronger as a small table or list so AI engines can extract the pattern cleanly. Consider: 'Attempt 1: 20s, Attempt 2: 40s, Attempt 3: 60s, Attempt 4: 80s' as a formatted list.

The Pricing callout after the opening paragraph is useful but vague on actual costs. If AWS Batch pricing numbers are stable enough to cite (e.g., Fargate per-vCPU-hour rate), add them. If not, the current framing is acceptable — just note that 'Costs depend on CPU, memory, and GPU requested' is less extractable than a concrete range.

Heading 'Resource connections' could be more search-aligned — rename to 'Connect to databases and other resources' or 'Database and resource access'. Queries like 'batch job connect to database' or 'batch job access S3 bucket' would benefit from a heading that matches those terms more directly.

The 'Logging' section is thin (one paragraph). Consider adding the default retention value more prominently ('90-day default retention') as a lead-in, and list the supported log forwarding destinations (Datadog, Highlight.io, HTTP endpoint) as a small bullet list so AI engines can extract supported integrations.

FAQ section is well-chosen and already present — good. Consider adding one more question: 'How much does a batch job cost?' pointing to Spot savings and the pay-per-run model. Cost questions are among the highest-intent queries for compute resources.

reviewerResults.persona: Small startup CTO with low to medium AWS experience. Cares most about time-to-production and shipping quickly without getting trapped in AWS complexity.
strengths: Excellent 'When to use' / 'Use something else' section with a concrete alternatives table — directly helps a startup CTO decide before committing to the resource.
Spot instances section is among the best guidance on the page: explicit when-to-use/when-to-avoid, pairs with retryConfig, and includes the SIGTERM/120s shutdown detail from the source.

Retry backoff formula and worked examples are accurate per runtime implementation (src/domain/.../batch-jobs/utils.ts:362-365), correctly identifying the behavior as linear rather than following the misleading 'exponential backoff' label in the JSDoc.
Comprehensive trigger table listing all 10 event types with concise use-case descriptions, plus a realistic multi-trigger code example showing both schedule and http-api-gateway.
Code examples properly use focus markers to highlight the relevant config section while keeping imports hidden — matches the intended CodeBlock pattern.

STP_TRIGGER_EVENT_DATA, STP_CURRENT_ATTEMPT, and STP_MAXIMUM_ATTEMPTS environment variables are documented and verified against the Step Functions state machine code — useful runtime detail that isn't obvious from the type definitions alone.
problems: Pricing line is vague ('Billed per vCPU and memory for the duration') compared to the example page which gives specific dollar figures. The provided Fargate pricing source incorrectly lists batch-job (batch jobs use EC2 via AWS Batch, confirmed in source), so specific numbers are hard to quote — but the page should at least acknowledge that pricing depends on the instance type AWS Batch selects.

Tab labels use 'TypeScript' while the human-reviewed example page consistently uses 'stacktape.ts'. The style guide says 'TypeScript' but the example page was approved with 'stacktape.ts' — creates inconsistency across the section.

The retryIntervalMultiplier JSDoc says 'exponential backoff' but the runtime implementation is linear (retryIntervalSeconds × attempt × multiplier). The draft correctly follows the implementation, but doesn't call out this JSDoc discrepancy — a reader who checks the types will be confused by the 'exponential backoff' label.

No mention of how to trigger a batch job manually (e.g., via the stateMachineArn referenceable parameter or stacktape invoke). A startup CTO testing a batch job will want to run it on demand before setting up event triggers.
mandatoryFixes: Add a sentence or short subsection explaining how to trigger a batch job on demand (e.g., reference the stateMachineArn or mention the CLI command), since a developer's first interaction with the resource will be manual testing, not waiting for a scheduled trigger.
optionalImprovements: Align tab labels with the example page ('stacktape.ts' instead of 'TypeScript') for cross-section consistency, or confirm which convention the section should follow.

Expand the pricing line to mention that costs depend on the EC2 instance type AWS Batch selects based on your cpu/memory/gpu requirements, and that Spot can reduce this significantly — even without exact dollar figures, framing helps the CTO budget.

In the Retries section, add a brief note that the JSDoc describes this as 'exponential backoff' but the actual behavior is linear scaling with attempt number, to prevent confusion for readers who cross-reference the types.
Add a one-liner about what happens when all retry attempts are exhausted (the Step Functions state machine transitions to a 'fail' state) so the reader knows the terminal behavior.

Consider moving the Environment Variables section after Connecting to Other Resources (or merging the connectTo env var explanation into it) to group all environment-variable-related content together and reduce the repeated mention of STP_* variables.

issues.statement: Pricing: Billed per vCPU and memory for the duration each job runs
issues.reason: The draft's pricing line implies Fargate-style vCPU/memory billing, but batch jobs use EC2 instances via AWS Batch, not Fargate. The authoritative pricing-info.ts file explicitly says pricing depends on 'the type of EC2 instances used by AWS' with an 'optimal' instance selection strategy. The pricing source file (@generated/aws-price/distilled/batch-job.md) incorrectly lists batch-job under Fargate — the runtime code at utils.ts:165 sets ComputeResources Type to 'EC2' or 'SPOT', never Fargate.
issues.suggestedFix: Rewrite the pricing line to reflect EC2 billing: 'Billed for EC2 instance time while your job runs. AWS Batch selects instance types to match your CPU, memory, and GPU requirements. Spot instances (`useSpotInstances`) can reduce costs by up to 90% for interruptible workloads.' Remove the 'per vCPU and memory' Fargate-style framing.

evidence.quote: The price of the batch-job depends on the length of the the job and on the type of EC2 instances used by AWS to execute your job (AWS uses "optimal" strategy to choose instance that match your job demands)

issues.statement: The `retryIntervalMultiplier` scales wait times linearly with the attempt number. The wait before attempt *n* is `retryIntervalSeconds × n × retryIntervalMultiplier`.
issues.reason: The formula in the draft is actually correct per the implementation (utils.ts:362-365), but the description 'scales wait times linearly with the attempt number' is misleading. The formula multiplies by the attempt number AND by the multiplier, which is not purely linear scaling — it's linear in attempt number but the multiplier acts as a constant factor. More importantly, the phrase 'before attempt *n*' is ambiguous: the computed examples use n=1,2,3,4 for the first 4 retries, but the config has `attempts: 5`, meaning there are 5 total attempts (1 initial + 4 retries). The draft says 'Before retry 1: 10 × 1 × 2 = 20s' through 'Before retry 4: 10 × 4 × 2 = 80s' which suggests 4 retries for 5 attempts — this is correct since the first attempt has no wait. However, calling it 'linear' when the type JSDoc says 'exponential backoff' may confuse users who read both.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the word 'linearly' to avoid confusion with the JSDoc. Simply describe the formula: 'The wait before retry *n* is `retryIntervalSeconds × n × retryIntervalMultiplier`.' This accurately reflects the implementation without contradicting the JSDoc terminology.

evidence.quote: Multiply wait time by this factor after each retry (exponential backoff).

issues.statement: AWS Batch selects from GPU instance families (`p4d` for A100, `g5` for A10G) based on your requirements.
issues.reason: The specific GPU instance families (p4d, g5) and their GPU models (A100, A10G) are not mentioned anywhere in the provided source files. The type definition only says 'Number of GPUs. The job will run on a GPU instance (NVIDIA A100, A10G, etc.)' without specifying instance families. The mapping of p4d→A100 and g5→A10G is general AWS knowledge but not grounded in the Stacktape source. verifierResults.summary: The draft is well-structured and mostly accurate, with strong grounding in source types. The most significant issue is the pricing line, which uses Fargate-style 'per vCPU and memory' framing when batch jobs actually use EC2 instances via AWS Batch (confirmed by both the implementation code and the authoritative pricing-info.ts). The retry backoff formula in the draft is actually correct per the implementation (linear: retryIntervalSeconds × n × retryIntervalMultiplier), though calling it 'linear' contradicts the JSDoc's 'exponential backoff' label — the JSDoc is what's inaccurate, but the draft should avoid the term to prevent confusion. The GPU instance family claims (p4d for A100, g5 for A10G) are not grounded in the source. All environment variables, trigger types, defaults, and component references are verified correct.

The draft is mostly aligned with the provided Batch Job type definitions, but it overstates several runtime behaviors and includes one clear retry-backoff error. The main factual risks are pricing/compute wording that conflicts with the provided pricing source, unsupported claims about Step Functions and injected runtime variables, and a trigger example that uses a resource name where the documented TypeScript example pattern suggests the rendered resource key is authoritative.

suggestions: The opening paragraph is strong and extractable as a Featured Snippet. One improvement: move the **Pricing** line into the opening paragraph itself (or immediately after the H1 as part of the same block) so LLMs that extract only the first paragraph also capture pricing context. Currently it's a separate bold-label block that some extractors may skip.

Add a compact FAQ section (3-5 questions) before the API reference. Natural candidates: 'What triggers can start a batch job?', 'How do Spot instances reduce batch job costs?', 'How do I retry a failed batch job?', 'Can I use GPUs with batch jobs?', 'What environment variables does a batch job receive?'. These map directly to long-tail queries and are already answered in the page — an FAQ section gives them a second, more extractable surface.

Shorten 'Connecting to other resources' heading to 'Resource connections' or 'Connect to resources'. At 31 characters it's fine for humans but shorter headings perform better as search result sitelinks and in AI-extracted outlines.

The Spot instances section mentions 'up to 90% discount' and '120 seconds' graceful shutdown — both excellent concrete claims. Add one more: state the default Spot allocation strategy if Stacktape/AWS Batch uses one (e.g., SPOT_CAPACITY_OPTIMIZED), so the paragraph is fully self-contained for an AI engine answering 'how do AWS Batch Spot instances work with Stacktape'.

The retries section has excellent concrete math examples. Consider adding a brief note about the maximum allowed value for `attempts` if one exists, so the paragraph answers 'what is the maximum retry count for a Stacktape batch job' without requiring the user to check the API reference.

The Logging section is thin compared to other sections. Add the default `retentionDays` value inline (you mention 90 days, good) and state whether CloudWatch log group names follow a predictable pattern (e.g., `/stacktape/{projectName}/{stage}/{resourceName}`). This makes the paragraph self-contained for ops-focused queries like 'where are Stacktape batch job logs stored'.
